title: The Needle Finder: Introduction to `grep`
description: Search for text patterns inside files. The hacker's most-used tool.

slides.title: Global Regular Expression Print: `grep` 🔍
slides.content:
# The Most Important Tool in Your Arsenal

`grep` searches through text files and prints lines that match a pattern you provide.

**Basic Syntax:**
```bash
grep "search term" filename
```

### Analogy: The Metal Detector 🏖️
Imagine a beach full of sand (the file). You want to find the metal coins (your search term). `grep` is the metal detector that scans every grain and beeps only when it finds metal.

**Example:**
```bash
grep "error" /var/log/syslog
```
This prints every line in `/var/log/syslog` that contains the word "error".

slides.title: Basic Search
slides.content: Create a sample file and find specific lines.
slides.codeSnippet:
echo -e "apple
banana
apple pie
orange" > fruits.txt
grep "apple" fruits.txt

quizData.question: What does `grep "password" file.txt` do?

options.text: Counts how many lines have the word 'password'.

quizData.explanation: `grep` stands for Global Regular Expression Print. It prints matching lines.

slides.title: Searching Multiple Files 📂
slides.content:
# One Command, Many Targets

`grep` can search through entire directories.

```bash
grep "TODO" *.txt
```
This searches all `.txt` files in the current directory for the word "TODO".

**Recursive Search:**
To search inside subdirectories as well, use the **`-r`** flag (Recursive).

```bash
grep -r "password" /home/user/Projects/
```
This is how hackers find hardcoded credentials in source code dumps. It's incredibly powerful.

slides.title: ⚠️ Case Sensitivity Matters
slides.content:
## `Error` vs `error`

By default, `grep` is **case-sensitive**.

*   `grep "error" log.txt` finds "error".
*   It **will not** find "Error" or "ERROR".

To ignore case, use the **`-i`** flag (Ignore case).

```bash
grep -i "error" log.txt
```

This is a lifesaver when you're not sure how the text was capitalized.

quizData.question: Which flag makes `grep` search through all files inside folders recursively?
quizData.explanation: `-r` stands for recursive. It dives into subdirectories.

slides.title: Inverting the Match: `-v` 🔄
slides.content:
# Find Lines That DO NOT Match

Sometimes finding what you're looking for is harder than excluding what you're NOT looking for.

The **`-v`** flag (Invert match) shows lines that **do not contain** the pattern.

**Example:**
```bash
grep -v "#" config.conf
```
This displays the config file but hides all the comment lines (lines starting with `#`). It shows you only the active settings.

slides.title: Pro Tip 🚀
slides.content:
## Piping into `grep`

You don't need a file! `grep` works perfectly with piped input.

```bash
ps aux | grep firefox
```

This checks if Firefox is running. We'll master pipes in Lesson 6.
